title: Chef Adek Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid
full_text: Chef Adek: The Many Lives Of Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid - Palate Asia Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Chef Adek: The Many Lives Of Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid 1 01 Chef Adek: The Many Lives Of Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid Main // Eat // Drink // Reviews // Insider // At Home // About Us // Download Our Magazine // Q2 2026 // Q1 2026 // Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 // Home Insider Chef Adek: The Many Lives Of Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid Chef Adek: The Many Lives Of Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid Colin Gomez At Home ,  Eat ,  Insider 4 months ago Share Datin Hamidah, also known as Chef Adek, has lived many lives: designer, homemaker, entrepreneur, and, eventually, one of Malaysia’s most respected chroniclers of its culinary traditions. This is the story of how those lives came to shape one another. Our lives are not one singular narrative, as we tend to view it, but rather a collection of different, smaller lives that coalesce into a whole only when viewed from a distance. Perhaps you used to be a dancer, before the tides of time gradually lapped at and eroded your bodily grace. Or maybe you were once an engineer who, one fine day, decided that you’d laid your eyes on a blueprint too many and then traded in your hard hat for an espresso machine and a portafilter. Such metamorphoses are common and are not to be viewed as a falling short, but rather as the natural conclusion of one season, making room for the next and lending the whole of a life greater depth for having moved on. One of my favourite such reinventions is the one undergone by the subject of this article, Datin Hamidah Abdul Hamid, fondly known as Chef Adek. These days, she may be more known for her deeply insightful cookbooks that detail treasured heritage recipes from her hometown state of Johor as well as across Malaysia, but what many might not know is that she was, at one time, one of the brightest stars in Kuala Lumpur’s nascent fashion scene of the 1980s—a designer with an uncommon touch whose creations were greatly sought after. When I first caught wind of her story, I was immediately intrigued—after all, how could I not be? I’ve witnessed my share of reinventions, but rarely have I encountered someone who has excelled so fully in two distinct lives. That sense of intrigue stayed with me as I travelled to her residence, quietly sequestered in a peaceful pocket of Bukit Jelutong, for an afternoon chat. I arrive outside her home, a low-slung house layered with terracotta roofs, screened behind palms and overreaching greenery. I ring the doorbell, and the automatic gates whirr to life before sliding away with a lazy mechanical chatter. Datin Hamidah greets me at the door and ushers me in with the easy warmth of a grandmother receiving company. We sit on her sofa and she offers me a drink. Introductions made, she settles comfortably into her seat. “So…” she says at last. “What would you like to know?” She clutches a notebook with an assemblage of scrawls, spanning one page and then halfway down the next. She’d come prepared, evidently, but I tell her that I’d much rather hear it from her off the cuff. It’s an approach that’s led to not just many a great interview for me, but full-blown conversations too. And so we agree to take it from the top. Our story begins many years ago, when Datin Hamidah, then a bright, starry-eyed young girl of 20, was all set to move to the UK to further her education and her own becoming. It was while staying at her cousin’s house there, in the midst of preparing for her move, that she would come face to face with the man who would remain by her side for the years to come. He was, at the time, a friend of her father’s and well known to the family. Datin Hamidah’s mother, in particular, was a big fan of the young man. Over the next few days, the man’s mother visited the house with a proposal: she’d like Datin Hamidah and her son to be wed. “This was 54 years ago and they were still matchmaking,” she explained. She ended up saying yes, but of course, it came at a cost. Accepting the proposal meant that all the dreams she’d dreamed, all the exciting turns she’d envisioned her life taking, would now all come to nought. “That was why I…” she begins slowly, before trailing off. She takes a moment to collect herself and continues. “Life just brought me to this, and I just followed, do you know what I mean? But I felt very restless, very useless, because I felt that had I furthered my studies, I knew I could’ve been somebody.” What she couldn’t have foreseen at the time, though, is that she would eventually go on to leave her mark on this world and become the somebody she’d always aspired to—just perhaps not quite in the ways she’d intended.
